content.full_text:
Cathedral
MAYOR PRO TEM
August 12, l99l
Fair Political Practices Commission
4281 Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814
Re: Request for Advice lrtter
Gentlemen:
I serve as an elected city councilmember of the City of Cathedral City, and as mayor pro tem of
the city.
I also own all or a major interest in two large parcels of land in the city, one being approximately
19 acres and one being approximately 22 acres.
One parcel is in an area of fast growth, on one of the city's most heavily traveled arterials, with
a shopping center having recently opened on land only l/4 mile distant. Presently, I have a hotel
with24 rooms available for rental on a l9-acre parcel, but have a plan on file now for a tentative
map separating out the hotel property and dividing the remaining parcel into approximately 30
small commercial or industrial parcels, on the order of a small industrial park. This property is
valued in excess of $2,000,000, but probably less than $4,000,000. The other parcel is on totally
undeveloped land lying northerly of Highway I-10, in an area where future growth of the city
may come, but which is not likely to develop for several years, minimum. Its value is in excess
of $1,500,000, but probably less than $2,500,000.
Both of my parcels have been for sale for an extended perid of time, and I have no intention
of personally developing the 19-acre site. My frling for a tentative map is a preliminary move
to help market the site, but I do not intend nor expect to actually develop any of thl sites,
individually or as a package.
Two years ago (June 21, 1989), the City Council adopted an ordinance known as the
Transportation Uniform Mitigation Fee Ordinance, which imposes charges for development of
properties of all kinds to offset capital charges to be incurred, on u regiJnal, Coachelli Valley-
wide basis, for transportation needs. Seven of the nine cities in the Coachella Valley, anA the
county of Riverside for its unincorporated area, adopted the fee.
:l
68-625 PEREZ ROAD'P.O, BOX 5OOl . CATHEDRAL CITY, CA 92235-5OOt o 619 / 324-B3BB. FAX: 6j9 / 324-4816


Fair Political Practices Commission
Page 2
August 12, 1991
The effect of the TUMF fee on property values is not known. Any fees which might apply on
development of my parcels would be paid by the developer of the land, however, not by me, as
I have no current intention of developing either of the parcels. As noted earlier, I am trying to
sell both of them, and have had them listed for sale for some time.
The amount of TUMF fees is based upon a formula set forth in the TUMF Handbook published
by the Coachella Valley Association of Governments, a copy of which I enclose for your
information. I am advised, as just one example, however, that the total TUMF fee imposed upon
developers of the approximately 15-acre shopping center near by l9-acre site was approximately
$241,000, which they were required to pay as a condition of development.
I had reservations about the TUMF ordinance from the start, due to my feelings that it could be
a tremendous restraint on commercial development. We have had regular reports on its impact,
and only recently has sufficient evidence been forthcoming that convinces me that it is indeed a
detriment to development.
I have recently publicly announced my concerns about this fee, as one other city moves to repeal
it in its jurisdiction. However, I am now alerted that another member of the City Council
suggests that, because of my ownership of property which could be subject to fees under the
TUMF ordinance, I may have a financial interest which precludes participation in anything
relating to the TUMF ordinance.
The TUMF ordinance was adopt€d by Cathedral City, the County of Riverside, and six other
cities in the Coachella Valley. One of our neighboring cities is now repealing the fee because
of what they find to be its adverse impact on developments in that city. I feel that Cathedral City
should look at similar repea.l.
However, if I do, in fact, have a disqualifying financial interest, the city attorney of cathedral
City indicates I may be restricted not only from raising the issue of such possible repeal with the
city council, but also lobbying for it, and perhaps even speaking out publicly for such repeal.
I therefore request informal advice, under the provisions of Regulation 1g329 (c), as to the
following:
1. 
As an owner of nvo rarge parcels of land in this city, predominantly undeveloped, may
I participate as a council member in discussions and ictions concerning trre rranslrtatlon
uniform Mitigation Fee ordinance, and its impact on growth and ievelopment of the
community?


2. 
If the answer to the first item is "no," and I am disqualified from participation as a
council member, may I nonetheless spea.k my mind publicly, to the newspaper reporters,
television news reporters, chambers of commerce, boards of realtors, building industry
associations, and civic organizations of interested citizens, without violating the Political
Reform Act of 1974, as amended, or do I surrender my own First Amendment rights,
even outside the confines of city Hall? I recognize that if I am disqualified from
participation as a council member, I must not actively participate in any matter directly
relating to the issue, and must not lobby city staff, the planning commission or other
public bodies of the city, or other members of the council, in any matter relating to the
issue, but must I also refrain from exercising my ordinary rights as a citizen and
landowner of this community?
If you need further information on this issue, please feel free to contact me dirert at my work
number, Ql3) 806-4&6.
Fair Political Practices Commission
Page 3
August 12, 1991
Enclosure
earliest possible time will be sincerely appreciated.
Allen Briggs, City Attomey
Your advice at
very
Pro Tem

sections.question:
1. As an owner of two large parcels of land in this city, predominantly undeveloped, may I participate as a council member in discussions and actions concerning the Transportation Uniform Mitigation Fee ordinance, and its impact on growth and development of the community?

2. If the answer to the first item is "no," and I am disqualified from participation as a council member, may I nonetheless speak my mind publicly, to the newspaper reporters, television news reporters, chambers of commerce, boards of realtors, building industry associations, and civic organizations of interested citizens, without violating the Political Reform Act of 1974, as amended, or do I surrender my own First Amendment rights, even outside the confines of city Hall?
sections.question_synthetic: A city councilmember and mayor pro tem who owns two large parcels of land in the city seeks advice on whether he has a disqualifying financial interest that would prohibit his participation in council discussions regarding the Transportation Uniform Mitigation Fee ordinance that could affect property values. He also asks whether, if disqualified from council participation, he can still speak publicly about the ordinance without violating the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response. The document contains two detailed numbered questions but no conclusion or advice from FPPC. The letter references Regulation 18329(c) and requests informal advice. No response or conclusion section exists in this document.

2. If the answer to the first item is "no," and I am disqualified from participation as a council member, may I nonetheless speak my mind publicly, to the newspaper reporters, television news reporters, chambers of commerce, boards of realtors, building industry associations, and civic organizations of interested citizens, without violating the Political Reform Act of 1974, as amended, or do I surrender my own First Amendment rights, even outside the confines of city Hall?

embedding.summary: A Cathedral City councilmember and mayor pro tem requests FPPC advice regarding potential conflict of interest stemming from his ownership of two large land parcels that could be subject to the Transportation Uniform Mitigation Fee ordinance.
